name: Volo's Guide to the Sword Coast
description: Volothamp Geddarm welcomes the reader to a survey of inns, taverns, shops, and sights along the western Heartlands — from south of Waterdeep to the borders of Amn, and east to Anauroch and Tunland.\n\nThe guide divides the Coast into five regions: the Coast proper, the High Moor, the Fields of the Dead, the Backlands, and Sunset Vale. Volo rates establishments with his trademark enthusiasm; marginal notes suggest Elminster has corrected more than a few claims.
author: Volothamp Geddarm (annotated by Elminster)

name: Volo's Guide to the North
description: The famed travelogue continues north of Waterdeep into the Savage Frontier — a land of long winters, proud city-states, and roads that vanish into monster-haunted wilds.\n\nVolo catalogues settlements from Luskan to Silverymoon, warns of orc holds and Zhentarim ambitions, and repeats traveller's wisdom on when to hire guides and when to simply run. Elminster's footnotes dispute several tavern prices.
author: Volothamp Geddarm (annotated by Elminster)

name: Volo's Guide to Waterdeep
description: An earlier volume in Volo's celebrated series, devoted entirely to the City of Splendors — its wards, guild politics, famous festhalls, and the unspoken rules of navigating noble society.\n\nVolo praises Waterdeep as the safest great city on the coast while cataloguing a dozen ways an outlander can still lose coin, dignity, or life before breakfast.
author: Volothamp Geddarm (annotated by Elminster)

name: Volo's Guide to All Things Magical
description: Volo turns from geography to enchantment — a gossip-laden tour of famous magic items, reputed spellbooks, and the eccentrics who collect them across the Heartlands.\n\nThe author insists he never steals secrets, only shares what any polite dinner guest might overhear. Several entries end with Elminster's terse corrections and warnings about cursed regalia.
author: Volothamp Geddarm (annotated by Elminster)

name: History of the North
description: A seven-volume chronicle tracing the North from the earliest human settlements through the rise and fall of kingdoms now remembered only in ruin and song.\n\nLater scholars treat it as foundational though incomplete — whole centuries along the Spine of the World are summarised in a single page, and several battles are attributed to heroes whose names no other source records.
author: Unknown

name: Traveler's Guide to the Sword Coast
description: A practical wayfarer's companion aimed at merchants and pilgrims rather than scholars — mile markers, toll customs, and which inns will actually honour a prepaid room.\n\nThe author advises that the Coast's main caravan routes are profitable precisely because they are dangerous, and that hiring armed escort is cheaper than recovering stolen wagons.
author: Unknown

name: The Dessarin River
description: A river trader's handbook describing the Dessarin from its headwaters in the Evermoors to the trade towns where barges turn goods toward Waterdeep and the Inner Sea.\n\nChapters cover seasonal floods, toll rights at ford towns, and which stretches are safe only when the Zhent patrols are paid and smiling.
author: Unknown

name: Quiet Days along the Dessarin
description: Leaontar Dlarvin records slow seasons on the river — empty quays, gossip in riverside taverns, and the small rituals that keep bargemen sane between cargoes.\n\nThe prose is gentle and observational: herons in the reeds, children fishing off piers, and the particular silence that falls when winter ice begins to creep south from the Surbrin.
author: Leaontar Dlarvin

name: A Sage's Travel Guide to the North
description: Zanshareen Sabiyryn wrote for fellow scholars who must leave their towers and cross the Frontier on foot — notes on safe campsites, reliable map sellers, and which lordlings will demand letters of introduction.\n\nThe guide is dry but precise, with marginal sketches of landmark trees and stone cairns used when snow hides the trail.
author: Zanshareen Sabiyryn

name: Famous Legends and Lore of the North
description: Harbalor Ilthon — called SnowSage in tavern verse — collects ballads, campfire tales, and disputed histories from Icewind Dale to the High Forest.\n\nEach legend is presented twice: the popular version sung in inns, and the older variant SnowSage claims predates human settlement. Readers are left to decide which is true.
author: Harbalor "SnowSage" Ilthon

name: Sayings of the North
description: A slim collection of proverbs gathered by Annath the Learned and Faeranduil of Neverwinter — terse counsel on weather, hospitality, steel, and silence.\n\n"Trust the dog that growls at its master before the stranger who smiles" is typical of the North's preference for blunt wisdom over courtly metaphor.
author: Annath the Learned and Faeranduil of Neverwinter

name: A Brief History of Undermountain
description: Daizez attempts a sober account of Halaster's mad labyrinth beneath Waterdeep — its layers, known entrances, and the expeditions that returned with maps, madness, or neither.\n\nThe author warns that any history of Undermountain is obsolete the moment it is inked, and dedicates a chapter to survivors' contradictory testimony.
author: Daizez

name: A Harper's Guide
description: Alustriel of Silverymoon compiled this work long before her elevation — a coded manual on recognizing Zhentarim fronts, safe houses, and the signs that a village song carries more than entertainment.\n\nCopies are rare outside Harper circles; the plain binding disguises a text that has saved more than one agent who memorised its ciphers.
author: Alustriel

name: A Wayfarer's Belt-Book of Advice
description: Rasthiavar of Iriaebor distilled decades of caravan work into pocket-sized counsel — how to pick guards, when to bribe tollkeepers, and why sleeping in armour saves more lives than any spell.\n\nThe belt-book is literally sized to tuck under a sword belt; margins are crowded with additions in other hands, suggesting it passed through many experienced travellers.
author: Rasthiavar of Iriaebor

name: A Historical Treatise of Dragonspear Castle
description: A military historian traces Dragonspear Castle from its founding as a fortified waystation through sieges, abandonment, and its recurring role as a rallying point for devils and cultists.\n\nThe treatise argues that whoever holds the castle controls not a fortress alone but the morale of every caravan crossing the Trade Way.
author: Unknown

name: Elminster's Travels Along the Sword Coast
description: The Old Mage records journeys taken when he still walked openly among mortals — observations on coastal politics, archmages in hiding, and the small kindnesses that keep civilisation from unraveling.\n\nThe tone shifts between travel diary and sermon; Elminster insists he wrote for future Harpers, not for publication, yet copies circulate in scriptoria from Baldur's Gate to Neverwinter.
author: Elminster

name: A Guide to the Cold North
description: Alagond of Tashluta wrote for merchants who treat the Frontier as a summer excursion — layering furs, judging ice thickness, and recognising the grey pallor that precedes fatal exposure.\n\nThe guide's appendix on frozen-ground burial rites is copied in nearly every caravan master's chest; southerners who skip it are often the ones found upright in snowdrifts come spring.
author: Alagond, sage of Tashluta

name: Trail Tales of the North
description: Jhalivar Thrunn collected first-person accounts from trappers, scouts, and refugees who crossed the Evermoors and lived — though not always intact — to tell of it.\n\nThe earliest edition at Candlekeep bears water stains and a note that two tales were added in a hand Thrunn disowned; readers still argue which stories he wished to suppress.
author: Jhalivar Thrunn

name: Secret Societies of the Sword Coast: Exposed
description: An anonymous pamphlet naming cults, trade cabals, and masked clubs operating from Baldur's Gate to Scornubel — with dates, meeting signs, and the author's repeated insistence that he expects assassination.\n\nSeveral entries are clearly wrong; others match Harper intelligence closely enough that scholars debate whether the writer was a traitor, a hero, or both.
author: Unknown

name: The North: Guide to the Savage Frontier
description: A comprehensive gazetteer produced by a council of sages — less colourful than Volo, more exhaustive on geography, demographics, and the political balance between Luskan, Neverwinter, and the dwarf holds.\n\nBoxed sets of maps once accompanied the text; surviving copies without maps still command high prices at Candlekeep.
author: Various sages of the North

name: A Brief History of the Dessarin
description: A frontier chronicle from Triboar traders — river commerce, orc raids through the Stone Bridge gap, and the slow spread of hamlets along the Long Road.\n\nThe author apologises for omitting elven histories prior to human logging, noting that some elders still refuse to speak of the valley's deeper past.
author: Anonymous scribe of Triboar

name: A Harper's Companion
description: Cressaed Wood compiled three volumes of practical counsel for Harper agents — ciphers, cover identities, and the unglamorous work of listening in market squares.\n\nThe third volume is said to list safe houses along the Long Road and the Dessarin; complete sets are kept under lock at Herald's Hall and Silverymoon's High Palace.
author: Cressaed Wood

name: A Harper's Song
description: Alustriel Silverhand's lyrical meditation on duty, loss, and the Harper code — part poetry, part memoir, and wholly unsuitable for public performance without training in coded verse.\n\nBards who recite it badly have been known to summon Harper auditors; those who recite it well sometimes receive invitations they cannot refuse.
author: Alustriel Silverhand

name: Arglauth's Guide to the North
description: Arglauth Melroryrr of Mulmaster wrote for eastern readers who trade with the Frontier but rarely travel it — a sober counterweight to Volo's colour and Elminster's asides.\n\nThe guide emphasises Zhentarim influence, Luskan's naval reach, and the economic choke points where a single burned bridge can starve a city.
author: Arglauth Melroryrr, Sage of Mulmaster

name: Architects Arcane: Waterdeep and the Sword Coast North
description: Paerl Nhesch documents warded foundations, floating spires, and the guild disputes that decide whether a tower may cast shadow on a neighbour's sun-court.\n\nWaterdeep's wards receive three chapters; Neverwinter's rebuilding after spellplague-era fires occupies a cautious appendix marked "subject to revision."
author: Paerl Nhesch

name: Annals of Baldur's Gate
description: A civic chronicle tracing Baldur's Gate from flint palisades to walled metropolis — duke successions, guild charters, and the recurring plague of patriar plots.\n\nLater editors appended Flaming Fist campaigns in the Outer City; purists insist the original four volumes end before the Fist became a power in its own right.
author: Unknown

name: The Book of the Coast
description: Mespert of Baldur's Gate assembled merchant contracts, harbour tariffs, and pirate amnesty proclamations into a single reference for factors sailing the Sword Coast trade.\n\nThe binding is utilitarian; margins swarm with later hands noting which clauses Lords' Alliance courts actually enforce.
author: Mespert of Baldur's Gate

name: The City of Neverwinter
description: An anonymous survey of Neverwinter's wards, noble houses, and the Mount Hotenow shadow that hangs over civic pride — written before the city's latest rebuilding.\n\nReaders comparing editions can trace disaster after disaster in the crossed-out street maps and hastily pasted addenda.
author: Unknown

name: History of Waterdeep
description: Six volumes of municipal history — from Ahghairon's founding compact through guild wars, noble lines, and the secret architectures of power beneath Mount Waterdeep.\n\nNo single author is credited; Candlekeep's copy shows three different scriptorium hands and a lacuna where a chapter on the Undermountain was razored out.
author: Unknown

name: Travels Along the Sword Coast
description: Elminster's earlier coastal diary — less sermon, more notebook — records inns where mages pay in secrets, pirates who honour guest-right, and the small customs that keep trade flowing when law fails.\n\nThe sage claims he never intended publication; the ink stains suggest otherwise.
author: Elminster of Shadowdale

name: Beware the Lizard Marsh!
description: Emythene Faldree warns travellers against the Lizard Marsh east of Daggerford — saurial ruins, black dragons, and the particular stupidity of camping on mud that breathes.\n\nThe pamphlet's woodcut illustrations are crude but accurate; several editions add a prayer to Talos that reads more like a curse.
author: Emythene Faldree

name: The Mysterious Harpers
description: Alamar Djelmuth's speculative history treats the Harpers as legend, conspiracy, and occasional nuisance — compiling rumours from taverns between Neverwinter and Amn.\n\nHarper agents reportedly bought and burned the first printing; surviving copies are prized by collectors who may not survive owning one.
author: Alamar Djelmuth

name: Why I Am a Harper
description: Belbradyn Tralaer's confessional explains why a successful merchant abandoned warehouses for coded songs and midnight rides — framed as advice to young idealists who confuse wealth with safety.\n\nThe final chapter lists debts Tralaer still owes in cities he can never visit openly again.
author: Belbradyn Tralaer

name: On the Matter of Magecraft and the City: A Summary for the Lords of Waterdeep
description: Khelben Arunsun the Elder briefed Waterdeep's Lords on arcane regulation — which magics threaten civic order, which guilds must be watched, and why some spells are best left unnamed in open council.\n\nThe summary remains classified in many editions; leaked copies circulate with whole pages replaced by single word: "No."
author: Khelben Arunsun ("the Elder")

name: The Neverwinter Wood
description: A naturalist's account of the forest east of Neverwinter — fey crossings, owlbear dens, and the Uthgardt taboos that forbid certain paths even in pursuit of game.\n\nThe author vanished mid-manuscript; the last pages describe lights that answered when called by name.
author: Unknown

name: Shields Aflame: A History of Kings and Battles of the North
description: Murtrosz Ondibran chronicles northern wars from Uthgardt moots to city-state alliances — with emphasis on how shield walls fail when commanders forget supply lines.\n\nBattle maps are stylised; veterans say the prose is accurate precisely where the illustrations lie.
author: Murtrosz Ondibran

name: Raging Blades: A Tale of the North
description: Halvidon Maeraed's ballad-cycle follows sellswords hired to escort a northern lord's bride — through blizzard, betrayal, and a duel fought on ice thin enough to sing.\n\nPerformed more often than read, the printed version restores verses censored in tavern renditions for fear of offending Zhent patrons.
author: Halvidon Maeraed, Bard of Elturel

name: Volo's Guide to the Dalelands
description: Volo turns east from the Coast to the Dalelands — feudal quirks, Harper-haunted forests, and the polite fiction that Dalesfolk are simple farmers rather than experienced guerrillas.\n\nElminster's notes are unusually extensive; several dale-lords reportedly offered bounties for the author's capture.
author: Volothamp Geddarm (annotated by Elminster)

name: Volo's Guide to Baldur's Gate
description: An unpublished manuscript circulated in samizdat — Volo's impressions of the Gate's patriars, Outer City grit, and which Flaming Fist officers accept bribes versus those who shoot first.\n\nVolo insists the work was suppressed by Duke's agents; critics note he never finished the chapter on the Undercellar.
author: Volothamp Geddarm

name: Sword Coast Songbook
description: Silke Rosena collected tavern songs from Luskan to Calimport's northern ports — work chanteys, pirate refrains, and love ballads that change lyrics by city to avoid bloodshed.\n\nA folded insert lists which verses start brawls in Waterdeep versus Baldur's Gate; prudent bards memorise both.
author: Silke Rosena

name: A Modest Guide to Sword Coast Wine
description: A vintner's primer on coastal appellations — where sea salt helps the grape, which Neverwinter cellars age well, and why Amnian exports command prices that have nothing to do with taste.\n\nThe guide's tasting notes are honest to a fault; one Waterdeep merchant sued over the entry on his house blend.
author: Unknown

name: On Goblins: My Life Among the Conquering Host
description: Volo recounts weeks embedded with a goblin war band — food, hierarchy, superstition, and the uncomfortable truth that many "conquering hosts" are starving families with stolen swords.\n\nElminster appended a warning that sympathy must not blur vigilance; goblins still bite.
author: Volothamp Geddarm

name: The Sharp Eye and Sharper Tongue: Observations of a Learned Sage
description: Ammathair Hawkfeather of Waterdeep documents civic absurdities — guild pride, noble feuds, and the arcane accidents that result when apprentices show off for crowds.\n\nThe tone is satirical but the facts check out; several subjects attempted to buy every copy and succeeded only in raising the price.
author: Ammathair Hawkfeather, Sage of Waterdeep

name: Codicillary Rules of Engagements for the Flaming Fist
description: The Flaming Fist's formal rules of engagement for mercenary contracts — escalation ladders, prisoner treatment, and when an officer may refuse an employer's order without court-martial.\n\nOutsiders find the codicil dry; soldiers in the Outer City treat selected passages as scripture.
author: Flaming Fist High Command

name: Tathaero's Short Sword Coast Chronicle
description: Aldreth Tathaero, scribe for hire, compiled a decade of coastal news — pirate captures, noble marriages, temple schisms, and one suspiciously detailed account of a red dragon over Triboar.\n\nTathaero disclaimers that accuracy follows payment; historians still cite the chronicle when better sources are silent.
author: Aldreth Tathaero, Scribe for Hire of Athkatla

name: Waterdeep – An Elven Diary
description: Malenya Brighteyes records centuries of Waterdeep as seen from immortal patience — seasons of human folly, brief friendships, and the ache of outliving every garden she planted.\n\nThe diary's elven script is annotated in Common by a later reader who marked passages "still true" with a trembling hand.
author: Malenya Brighteyes

name: Known Networks of the North
description: A trader's map in prose — overland routes, river links, and smuggling paths through the Evermoors that official caravans pretend not to know.\n\nThe thirteenth-century original is updated in margins by generations of merchants; some notes are in cipher, some in blood.
author: Unknown

name: Lines on the North
description: Hommil Dundreth, merchant-mage of Hlintar, versifies trade wisdom — when to buy furs, when to sell steel, and how winter turns copper into gold for those who survive it.\n\nThe poem is taught in counting-houses; Dundreth's enemies claim he hid loan-shark rates in the metre.
author: Hommil Dundreth, Merchant-Mage of Hlintar

name: Luskan Threat to Neverwinter
description: An intelligence brief on Luskan's naval ambitions — ship counts, Hosttower meddling, and the historical pattern of winter raids on Neverwinter's harbour.\n\nThe author is unnamed; Lord Neverember's seal appears on some copies, suggesting official sponsorship and selective distribution.
author: Unknown

name: Balduran Founds a City
description: Legend and municipal pride intertwine in this account of Balduran's voyages and the founding of Baldur's Gate — sea devils, buried treasure, and the first walls raised against the Sword Coast's chaos.\n\nHistorians note the text mixes myth with charter language; citizens note it reads well at Founders' Day feasts.
author: Unknown

name: Order of the Gauntlet: On Matters of Law and Justice
description: A doctrinal text for Gauntlet knights — when justice requires the sword, when mercy serves the righteous, and how to investigate evil without becoming what you hunt.\n\nFrontier chapters address orc raids, Zhent slavery, and cooperation with Flaming Fist garrisons that do not share the Order's oaths.
author: Order of the Gauntlet scribes

name: Volo's Guide to Cormyr
description: Volo's account of Cormyr's court etiquette, Purple Dragon deployments, and the polite fiction that the Forest Kingdom is less dangerous than the Frontier.\n\nElminster's footnotes suggest Volo was escorted out of Suzail faster than he entered; the guide's tone is admiring and terrified in equal measure.
author: Volothamp Geddarm (annotated by Elminster)

name: Aurora's Whole Realms Catalogue
description: Aurora's famous mail-order catalogue lists goods from across Faerûn — prices, shipping times, and the occasional item whose description understates its magical properties.\n\nFrontier traders use it as a price index; collectors hunt misprinted editions where Aurora forgot to mention that a "gentleman's grooming kit" includes a wand.
author: Aurora

name: History of Daggerford
description: History of Daggerford — A chronicle preserved in northern and coastal scriptoria — dates, names, and the sort of detail merchants pretend not to need. Library catalogues list no certain author; the work is dated to before 1372 DR.\n\nLater hands added marginal dates and crossed out names; historians still argue which revisions were propaganda.
author: Unknown

name: Illefarn
description: Illefarn — A chronicle preserved in northern and coastal scriptoria — dates, names, and the sort of detail merchants pretend not to need. Library catalogues list no certain author; the work is dated to before 1372 DR.\n\nLater hands added marginal dates and crossed out names; historians still argue which revisions were propaganda.
author: Unknown

name: Legends of Icewind Dale
description: Legends of Icewind Dale — Legends collected from tavern verse, elder memory, and sources no scholar will swear to in court. Library catalogues list no certain author; the work is dated to before 1312 DR.\n\nEach generation adds a verse; purists claim the oldest layer is true and everything since is flattery.
author: Unknown

name: Ghost of Conyberry
description: Ghost of Conyberry — Legends collected from tavern verse, elder memory, and sources no scholar will swear to in court. Library catalogues list no certain author; the work is dated to before 1372 DR.\n\nEach generation adds a verse; purists claim the oldest layer is true and everything since is flattery.
author: Unknown

name: Gorgomorz - Daggerford's Devil
description: Gorgomorz - Daggerford's Devil — A chronicle preserved in northern and coastal scriptoria — dates, names, and the sort of detail merchants pretend not to need. Hilgraff the Scribe is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to 1270–1372 DR.\n\nLater hands added marginal dates and crossed out names; historians still argue which revisions were propaganda.
author: Hilgraff the Scribe

name: Darkness over Daggerford
description: Darkness over Daggerford — A chronicle preserved in northern and coastal scriptoria — dates, names, and the sort of detail merchants pretend not to need. Library catalogues list no certain author; the work is dated to 1372 DR.\n\nLater hands added marginal dates and crossed out names; historians still argue which revisions were propaganda.
author: Unknown

name: Trade Way Travel Stops
description: Trade Way Travel Stops — Practical notes for wayfarers — tolls, seasons, inns, and the unwritten rules of safe passage. Library catalogues list no certain author; the work is dated to before 1372 DR.\n\nExperienced caravans treat the counsel as suggestion only — but rookies who ignore it seldom return to disagree.
author: Unknown

name: The Trade of Blades
description: The Trade of Blades — Commercial wisdom dressed as prose — prices, routes, guild customs, and when to walk away from a deal. Augustavus Cale is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to before 1372 DR.\n\nFactors along the Long Road keep folded excerpts in strongboxes next to their scales and seals.
author: Augustavus Cale

name: Tales Told by the Warm Fireside
description: Tales Told by the Warm Fireside — A popular tale in print — performed in taverns before it was ever bound between covers. Korin of Neverwinter is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to 1334 DR.\n\nCritics call it low art; innkeepers call it excellent for business on rainy nights.
author: Korin of Neverwinter

name: One Warrior's Life
description: One Warrior's Life — A chronicle preserved in northern and coastal scriptoria — dates, names, and the sort of detail merchants pretend not to need. Azlundar, Lion of Neverwinter is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to 1289 DR.\n\nLater hands added marginal dates and crossed out names; historians still argue which revisions were propaganda.
author: Azlundar, Lion of Neverwinter

name: The Rise of Randral
description: The Rise of Randral — A popular tale in print — performed in taverns before it was ever bound between covers. Sandreth Yendrel of Neverwinter is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to 1326 DR.\n\nCritics call it low art; innkeepers call it excellent for business on rainy nights.
author: Sandreth Yendrel of Neverwinter

name: Tavern Dancing My Way to Wizardly Might
description: Tavern Dancing My Way to Wizardly Might — A treatise on magic as it is actually practiced — wards, reputations, and the price of mispronunciation. Jerenneth Ghaluin of Neverwinter is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to 1349 DR.\n\nComplete copies are rare; excerpted versions circulate among mages who insist the missing pages do not matter.
author: Jerenneth Ghaluin of Neverwinter

name: Of the Wonders of Magery
description: Of the Wonders of Magery — A treatise on magic as it is actually practiced — wards, reputations, and the price of mispronunciation. Albaerum the Sage of Neverwinter is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to 1320s DR.\n\nComplete copies are rare; excerpted versions circulate among mages who insist the missing pages do not matter.
author: Albaerum the Sage of Neverwinter

name: Notes from a Soothsayer
description: Notes from a Soothsayer — A treatise on magic as it is actually practiced — wards, reputations, and the price of mispronunciation. Jeraldine Haverlowe is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to before 1492 DR.\n\nComplete copies are rare; excerpted versions circulate among mages who insist the missing pages do not matter.
author: Jeraldine Haverlowe

name: Musings of a Shameless Noble
description: Musings of a Shameless Noble — A text that names names — or appears to — and has outlived more than one owner. Lady Citta Hothemer of Waterdeep is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to 1357 DR.\n\nSeveral entries end mid-sentence, as if the author learned something and chose discretion over royalties.
author: Lady Citta Hothemer of Waterdeep

name: Forty Years Before the Doors
description: Forty Years Before the Doors — A chronicle preserved in northern and coastal scriptoria — dates, names, and the sort of detail merchants pretend not to need. Zanthravas Rolovantar is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to 1363 DR.\n\nLater hands added marginal dates and crossed out names; historians still argue which revisions were propaganda.
author: Zanthravas Rolovantar

name: Falcon Fun
description: Falcon Fun — Natural history from hunters, riverfolk, and sages who went outside and wrote down what bit them. Edwin Narlok of Waterdeep is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to 1344 DR.\n\nSeasonal addenda in surviving copies suggest the author revised the work every year they lived to see spring.
author: Edwin Narlok of Waterdeep

name: Lords' Writ
description: Lords' Writ — A chronicle preserved in northern and coastal scriptoria — dates, names, and the sort of detail merchants pretend not to need. Ahghairon is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to 1039 DR.\n\nLater hands added marginal dates and crossed out names; historians still argue which revisions were propaganda.
author: Ahghairon

name: Hilather's Workbook
description: Hilather's Workbook — A treatise on magic as it is actually practiced — wards, reputations, and the price of mispronunciation. Halaster Blackcloak is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to circa 132 DR.\n\nComplete copies are rare; excerpted versions circulate among mages who insist the missing pages do not matter.
author: Halaster Blackcloak

name: Fallen Legacies: Arcaeological and Historical Analysis of the Mere of Dead Men
description: Fallen Legacies: Arcaeological and Historical Analysis of the Mere of Dead Men — A chronicle preserved in northern and coastal scriptoria — dates, names, and the sort of detail merchants pretend not to need. Khelben "Blackstaff" Arunsun is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to 1346 DR.\n\nLater hands added marginal dates and crossed out names; historians still argue which revisions were propaganda.
author: Khelben "Blackstaff" Arunsun

name: Harpering by Moonlight: Approaches to Life
description: Harpering by Moonlight: Approaches to Life — Harper-related lore — coded, partial, and dangerous to quote without looking over your shoulder. Elminster of Shadowdale is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to 1330s DR.\n\nNon-Harper readers report the prose is readable; Harper readers report it is a test.
author: Elminster of Shadowdale

name: Slumbertime Stories for Sprigs
description: Slumbertime Stories for Sprigs — A popular tale in print — performed in taverns before it was ever bound between covers. Syluné of Shadowdale is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to 1330s DR.\n\nCritics call it low art; innkeepers call it excellent for business on rainy nights.
author: Syluné of Shadowdale

name: Translations from Kara-Tur Haiku
description: Translations from Kara-Tur Haiku — Practical notes for wayfarers — tolls, seasons, inns, and the unwritten rules of safe passage. Lhaeo of Shadowdale is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to 1354 DR.\n\nExperienced caravans treat the counsel as suggestion only — but rookies who ignore it seldom return to disagree.
author: Lhaeo of Shadowdale

name: Grand History of the Realms
description: Grand History of the Realms — A chronicle preserved in northern and coastal scriptoria — dates, names, and the sort of detail merchants pretend not to need. Elminster is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to 15th century DR.\n\nLater hands added marginal dates and crossed out names; historians still argue which revisions were propaganda.
author: Elminster

name: The High History of Faerûnian Archmages Mighty
description: The High History of Faerûnian Archmages Mighty — A treatise on magic as it is actually practiced — wards, reputations, and the price of mispronunciation. Antarn the Sage is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to 1366 DR.\n\nComplete copies are rare; excerpted versions circulate among mages who insist the missing pages do not matter.
author: Antarn the Sage

name: Planar Travels: A Study of the Planes of Good
description: Planar Travels: A Study of the Planes of Good — A treatise on magic as it is actually practiced — wards, reputations, and the price of mispronunciation. Elminster is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to before 1371 DR.\n\nComplete copies are rare; excerpted versions circulate among mages who insist the missing pages do not matter.
author: Elminster

name: Volo's Guide to the Moonsea
description: Volo's Guide to the Moonsea — A regional survey written for travellers who cannot afford to learn geography from dead companions. Volothamp Geddarm is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to 1357 DR.\n\nMaps are absent or obsolete in most copies; readers are expected to update the text with their own ink.
author: Volothamp Geddarm

name: Volo's Guide to the Vast
description: Volo's Guide to the Vast — A regional survey written for travellers who cannot afford to learn geography from dead companions. Volothamp Geddarm is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to 1358 DR.\n\nMaps are absent or obsolete in most copies; readers are expected to update the text with their own ink.
author: Volothamp Geddarm
